alternative_translations: mental and physical objects of consciousness
definition: The pairing of name and form, or the mental and physical phenomena structured within experience.
notes: The project keeps name-and-form as the default because it preserves the compact doctrinal structure of the compound without collapsing it too quickly into a paraphrase. Depending on context, the term may be unpacked as naming and materiality or as mental and physical phenomena or objects of consciousness. The term should be heard as an analytic pairing within experience, especially in the mutual dependence of name-and-form and knowing. Translators should not silently replace the doctrinal compound with mind and body in core dependent-arising passages unless the broader explanatory goal is made explicit in the surrounding prose.

context_rules.context: In most doctrinal and dependent-origination contexts
In explanatory prose where the processive or functional sides need to be made explicit
context_rules.notes: Use when the active naming side of nāma should be heard more clearly.

context_rules.context: When the passage emphasizes the paired structuring of experience in dependent origination
context_rules.notes: Preserve the doctrinal compactness of the canonical compound.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose unpacking the pair for modern readers
context_rules.notes: Acceptable alternate when a broader explanatory gloss is helpful.

context_rules.context: When the passage is explaining nāmarūpa as the mental and physical objects related to knowing
context_rules.rendering: mental and physical objects of consciousness
context_rules.notes: Use only where that explanatory framing is specifically intended.

example_phrases.translation: with name-and-form as condition, sense field
example_phrases.notes: Shows the downstream role of the term in the chain.

version_notes: Strengthened to keep the compound stable in dependent-arising contexts and to block casual collapse into mind-and-body paraphrase.
